---
title: "COP31 Simulation — Facilitator Guide"
subtitle: "Lecture 14 | PA 388K/345 Climate Policy"
author: "Andrew Waxman"
date: "April 22, 2026"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry: margin=1in
    colorlinks: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
---

\newpage

# Before Class

**Room setup:** Arrange tables so each country has a clear position. No assigned seating within countries — students sit where they like. You need a clear path between tables for bilateral diplomacy.

**Tool:** Open <https://www.andrewrwaxman.com/climate-agreements-sim-expo/> on your laptop, projected for the class. Expand the US Shock Panel and Leverage Table instructor panels before class — check that both work. Keep both **collapsed** when students arrive.

**Distribute:** Proposal forms (two per student — one per round) and the student rules sheet. Students should have read the rules sheet before class from L13 distribution.

**Have ready:** A whiteboard or flip chart to record proposals during plenary presentations (or use the tool's Quick Entry modal).

---

# Session Arc — Time Budget

| Block | Time | Running total |
|---|---|---|
| SG Opening | 15 min | 0:15 |
| Coalition Caucus | 8 min | 0:23 |
| Opening Statements | 20 min | 0:43 |
| Round 1 Bilateral | 15 min | 0:58 |
| Round 1 Plenary + Audit + Model | 10 min | 1:08 |
| **10-minute break** | 10 min | 1:18 |
| US Shock Announcement | 3 min | 1:21 |
| Round 2 Bilateral | 12 min | 1:33 |
| Round 2 Plenary + Audit + Model | 10 min | 1:43 |
| **5-minute break** | 5 min | 1:48 |
| Debrief + Course Wrap | 50 min | 2:38 |
| *Buffer* | 22 min | 3:00 |

---

\newpage

# Part 1 — Secretary General Opening (15 min)

*You are the UN Secretary General throughout the simulation. Stay in role during all simulation phases. Step out of role only during debrief.*

## Opening Script

> "The Conference of the Parties to the UNFCCC, its thirty-first session, is called to order.
>
> We meet in Antalya at a moment of considerable gravity. Current global policies place us on a trajectory toward approximately 2.7 degrees of warming by 2100. The commitments made in this room over the next two hours will determine whether we close that gap — or whether we confirm that the gap cannot be closed.
>
> I want to name something before we begin. Some delegations at this table control far more of the temperature outcome than others. That is not a flaw in this process — it is why these negotiations are hard. China's emissions are roughly forty percent of what this table can influence. Marshall Islands and Fiji together are a fraction of a percent. That asymmetry is the problem you are here to solve.
>
> The question is not only what the largest emitters commit to. It is whether the countries most at risk of catastrophic outcomes can create conditions under which those emitters are willing to move — and whether the countries with the greatest historical responsibility are willing to pay for the transition they made necessary.
>
> One further note: the United States is not at this table. The United States accounts for approximately eighteen percent of global emissions and is currently following a business-as-usual trajectory. Its absence is a fact of this negotiation. Whether that changes during our session remains to be seen.
>
> The goal is clear: an agreement that limits warming to 2 degrees Celsius, achieves sufficient participation, and is credible enough to implement. The model before you will tell you whether your proposals meet that standard. It will not tell you what is fair. That is your job.
>
> Let us begin."

## After the Script — Tool Orientation (5 min)

Show the tool on the projector.

1. Show the BAU scenario — point to the temperature metric: approximately 2.7°C.
2. Enter one hypothetical country (e.g., China at 5%/yr starting 2030) — show the temperature drop. Reset immediately.
3. Show the three parameters: start year, rate, transfer.
4. Point to the Net Transfer Balance indicator — explain it must balance to zero when proposals are entered.
5. *Do not* show the Leverage Table or US Shock Panel.

---

\newpage

# Part 2 — Coalition Caucus (8 min)

_Two Options:_

## Option A — Structured: Announce groups and ask them to meet. Groups:

Announce groups and ask them to meet. Groups:

| Group | Countries |
|---|---|
| High Emitters | Japan, EU |
| Emerging Major Emitters | Brazil, China, India |
| Fossil Fuel Exporters | Nigeria, Norway |
| Climate-Vulnerable States | Fiji, Marshall Islands |
| Other Developing | Vietnam, Mexico |

Distribute one index card to each group as they sit down. Tell them the card is due before opening statements begin. Three fields only — write them on the board:

> **1. Opening ask** (start year range, rate range, or transfer floor/ceiling — specific enough to enter in a proposal form)
> **2. Priority bilateral partner** (one country outside your group you need to talk to first)
> **3. Red line** (one thing your group will not accept)

Timer: use the tool's 8-minute preset.

Circulate but do not intervene. Observe which groups converge quickly and which struggle — the struggle is data. Fossil Fuel Exporters (Norway/Nigeria) and Climate-Vulnerable States (Fiji/Marshall Islands) will likely have the hardest time producing a unified card; that's expected and pedagogically useful, not a facilitation failure.

Collect cards before calling opening statements. Do not read them aloud. Use them during the simulation to track whether groups are negotiating toward their stated red lines and whether bilateral diplomacy follows the priority partners they identified. Reference them selectively in the debrief — e.g., *"Your group's card said your red line was X. Did you hold it? What happened when you didn't?"*

If a group cannot agree on one or more fields, they write "no consensus" — that is an acceptable and informative answer.


Announce groups and ask them to meet. Groups:

| Group | Countries |
|---|---|
| High Emitters | Japan, EU |
| Emerging Major Emitters | Brazil, China, India |
| Fossil Fuel Exporters | Nigeria, Norway |
| Climate-Vulnerable States | Fiji, Marshall Islands |
| Other Developing | Vietnam, Mexico |

Circulate. Do not intervene. Observe who is already reaching across groups.

Timer: use the tool's 8-minute preset.

## Option B — Free-form: Announce bilateral diplomacy starts immediately. Encourage students to find their own allies and negotiate from the start.
 
 
**Before starting the timer**, pose one question aloud to the full room:

> *"Is your country's opening emissions commitment conditional on receiving financial transfers — or are you prepared to make a commitment independent of what finance flows?"*

Give students 30 seconds to decide. Most will know immediately from their negotiation brief preparation. Direct each group to a corner of the room:

- **Finance-conditional** (commitment requires transfers as a precondition): Marshall Islands, Fiji, Nigeria, India, Vietnam, Brazil, Mexico — expect 6–7 students
- **Finance-independent** (prepared to commit regardless of transfers): Japan, EU, Norway, China — expect 3–4 students

Students self-sort. Do not assign anyone. If a student is genuinely uncertain, tell them to join whichever group reflects their opening position in their negotiation brief — they can shift during bilateral diplomacy.

Distribute one index card to each group. Write the three fields on the board:

> **1. Opening ask** — a specific transfer floor (conditional group) or transfer ceiling/offer (independent group), specific enough to enter in a proposal form
> **2. Priority bilateral partner** — one country outside your caucus group you need to talk to in the first five minutes of Round 1
> **3. Red line** — one thing your group will not accept in any agreement

Start the **8-minute timer**.

**Your role during the caucus:** circulate but do not intervene. Observe which groups converge and which struggle. The finance-conditional group will likely have productive internal conversation about their collective transfer floor — that's useful intelligence. The finance-independent group is smaller and may converge quickly; prompt them with *"What are you collectively willing to offer, and to whom?"* if they run out of agenda.

If any group cannot agree on a field, "no consensus" is an acceptable answer. Do not resolve the disagreement for them.

Collect cards before calling opening statements. Do not read them aloud. Use them for two purposes:

1. **During the simulation:** track whether countries negotiate toward or away from their stated red lines; note whether bilateral diplomacy follows the priority partners each group identified
2. **During the debrief:** reference selectively — *"Your group's card said your red line was X — did you hold it? What made you move or not move?"* This connects the caucus output to the framing shift discussion in the debrief

**One note:** the caucus does not create binding negotiating blocs. Brazil and Norway can negotiate directly the moment bilateral diplomacy opens. The self-sort is a starting position and a brief introduction to the students whose strategic situation most resembles yours — nothing more. Make this explicit to the room before starting the timer: *"This caucus is preparation. Bilateral diplomacy is fully open — you can talk to anyone in any order once it begins."*

---

# Part 3 — Opening Statements (20 min)

Call each delegation to present. Formal tone — *"The Secretary General calls on the delegation of [Country] to address the conference."*

**Order:**

1. Marshall Islands
2. Fiji
3. Vietnam
4. Mexico
5. Nigeria
6. Norway
7. Japan
8. EU
9. Brazil
10. India
11. China

Enforce 2 minutes per country. Record key positions on the whiteboard — start year ranges, transfer demands, stated red lines. This record becomes the baseline for the condition audit.

---

\newpage

# Part 4 — Round 1 Bilateral Diplomacy (15 min)

Start the 15-minute timer. Step back — your role during bilateral is to observe, answer procedural questions, and be ready to manage Loss and Damage Moments.

## Loss and Damage Moment Protocol

If Fiji or Marshall Islands announces a Loss and Damage Moment:

1. Call the room to order immediately: *"The Secretary General recognizes a Loss and Damage Moment. All bilateral negotiations pause."*
2. Give the invoking country the floor for exactly 90 seconds. Do not allow interruption.
3. After 90 seconds: *"The Loss and Damage Moment concludes. Bilateral diplomacy resumes."*
4. Note on your record sheet that this student has used their invocation.

## Last 2 Minutes

Announce: *"Two minutes remaining. Complete your proposal forms."*

---

# Part 5 — Round 1 Plenary Presentations + Condition Audit + Model Entry (10 min)

## Plenary Presentations (6 min)

Same order as opening statements. Each country states:

- Start year and reduction rate
- Any conditions on the commitment (stated precisely)
- Net transfer number
- Key bilateral agreements reached

Record all proposals on the whiteboard as countries present. Flag conditional pledges with a asterisk.

## Condition Audit (2 min)

Work through each conditional pledge in order. For each:

1. State the condition publicly: *"[Country] conditioned on [specific parameter]. The bilateral agreements on record show [amount]. Condition is [met / not met by $X B/yr]."*
2. If not met, ask the conditioner: *"[Country], your condition is unmet. Do you relax and commit at your conditional rate, or do you submit your fallback?"*
3. Country states choice out loud.
4. If fallback triggers a second condition, audit that immediately in the same pass.

## Model Entry (2 min)

Use Quick Entry modal for speed. Enter all proposals. If any country is still finalizing (cascade resolution), enter BAU for that country and update immediately after.

Show results. Announce the temperature: *"The proposals on this table produce [X]°C by 2100. The 2°C target requires [Y]°C more reduction. We are [above / below] the target."*

Navigate to Costs & Damages tab — this is now visible to students for Round 2.

---

\newpage

# Part 6 — 10-Minute Break

While students are on break:

- Expand the US Shock Panel on your laptop (not on projector)
- Confirm Shock A parameters are set: New president re-entry + net zero 2050
- Prepare your announcement script (below)
- Review the bilateral agreements recorded — note any conditions that almost cascaded; these are worth naming in debrief

---

# Part 7 — US Shock Announcement (3 min)

After break, before bilateral resumes. Stay in SG role.

## Shock A Script

> "The Secretary General wishes to inform delegations of a development that occurred during our recess.
>
> The newly inaugurated President of the United States has announced that the United States will rejoin the UNFCCC and submit a nationally determined contribution committing to net-zero greenhouse gas emissions by 2050. The administration has indicated it will seek Senate ratification and proposes beginning emissions reductions in 2028.
>
> The United States remains outside this negotiating room. It has not offered transfer payments. It has not sought verification arrangements with other parties. It has made a unilateral commitment.
>
> Delegations are invited to adjust their Round 2 positions accordingly."

Now activate Shock A on the tool (US Shock Panel — switch from BAU to Shock A). The temperature on the projector will update slightly. Do not draw attention to the magnitude of the shift — let students notice it themselves during Round 2.

---

# Part 8 — Round 2 Bilateral Diplomacy (12 min)

Same procedure as Round 1. No coalition caucus this round — free-form bilateral immediately.

Reminder: any Loss and Damage Moments not yet used remain available.

---

\newpage

# Part 9 — Round 2 Plenary + Audit + Model Entry (10 min)

Same procedure as Round 1. After model entry:

Show final temperature. Then show Financial Summary tab — basic version (abatement costs, transfers, damages as % GDP/cap). **Do not reveal the debrief breakdown yet** — the "Show Detailed Breakdown" button should not be clicked until the debrief.

*"These are your Round 2 commitments. The temperature is [X]°C. We will examine what this means — and what it cost each party — in the debrief."*

---

# Part 10 — 5-Minute Break

Reset your facilitation mode — you are stepping out of SG role for the debrief. Before break ends, expand the Leverage Table panel on your laptop and confirm all three reveal states are working.

---

\newpage

# Part 11 — Debrief (50 min)

*Out of SG role from here. Speak as instructor.*

## Step 1 — Immediate Reactions (8 min)

Do not open with a question. Ask students to turn to a neighbor for 2 minutes: *"How do you feel about what just happened?"* Then share with the group.

Acknowledge feelings before analysis. Listen for: frustration, pride, resignation, surprise. Do not redirect to analysis yet — let the room sit with the emotional register of the experience.

## Step 2 — Framing Shift (10 min)

Return to the Round 1 temperature reveal moment.

> "After Round 1, I asked: does seeing that number change anyone's calculus? I want to go back to that. Did anyone shift from a distributive frame — 'you caused this, you pay' — toward an integrative frame — 'we all lose if this fails'? What produced that shift, if it happened?"

If the shift happened: name it. Distributive vs. integrative bargaining. The model feedback is what made integrative framing rational — without seeing the collective consequence, there's no reason to move off your national position.

If the shift did not happen: that's also data worth naming. Why not? Was the asymmetry too large? Was the US absence too demoralizing? Was the condition audit too disruptive?

## Step 3 — Leverage Table Reveal (8 min)

Expand the Leverage Table on the projector.

**First reveal — emissions column only:**

> "Let's look at the emissions structure of this room."

Show the table sorted by emissions. Ask: *"China controls roughly forty percent of what this room can influence. Marshall Islands and Fiji together control a fraction of a percent. Given that — how should we evaluate what Miles and Anisa accomplished today? What tools did they actually have?"*

Let students respond. Then:

**Second reveal — temperature leverage column:**

> "Now let me show you what the emissions translate to in terms of temperature leverage — how much each country can actually move the thermometer with the most aggressive cut possible."

Show the full leverage table. Ask: *"What does this tell you about the design of this negotiation? And what does it tell you about why Loss and Damage exists as a concept in the real negotiations?"*

## Step 4 — Rest-of-World Reveal (5 min)

> "Now let me show you something we haven't talked about yet. Everything you negotiated today covers roughly sixty percent of global emissions. Here's what the rest of the world does."

Enter Rest-of-World at BAU using the tool (manually enter Russia ~2.8 Gt at BAU growth, Indonesia ~2.5 Gt, Saudi Arabia ~0.9 Gt, Canada ~0.8 Gt as additional inputs — or describe verbally while pointing to the ROW panel in the Leverage Table).

Show the temperature tick upward.

> "Your deal was real. What this room agreed to was real. And here is what happens when Russia, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, and Canada stay on their current trajectory regardless of what you decided. What mechanism would have needed to exist to bring them to this table — and what would it have cost?"

## Step 5 — Trilemma Synthesis (10 min)

Write the three legs on the board: **Ambition — Participation — Compliance.**

Work through each:

**Ambition:** *"What temperature did you reach? What would it have taken to hit 2°C — and who would have had to move most?"* Point to China's leverage number. *"Did Ryan have enough information and enough incentive to move further? What would have changed that?"*

**Participation:** *"Who almost walked out? What were the conditions for staying? Did the US absence make agreement easier or harder — and why?"* Note that Shock A moved the temperature by only 0.12°C. *"The most ambitious US climate commitment in history. What does that tell you about the structure of the collective action problem?"*

**Compliance:** *"You entered numbers into a model. There was no enforcement mechanism. Would your commitments have been kept? What did Ostrom say about the conditions under which self-governance works — and how many of those conditions were present in this room today?"*

## Step 6 — Course Arc (9 min)

Work backward through the semester explicitly. Connect each major theme to what students just experienced:

- The bilateral transfer negotiations they just ran — that's the CBDR and Broome rights framework from L9 made operational
- The condition audit — that's the MRV/accountability content from L8 in miniature (what you can verify, you can enforce; what you cannot verify, you cannot condition on)
- The coalition dynamics — Olson and Ostrom from L13 in practice
- The framing of opening statements — the communication and agenda-setting content from L10
- The damage asymmetry that made Marshall Islands and Fiji's position so different from Norway's — that's the welfare economics of L3 and the ethics framework of L9
- The aggregate/local tension from L12 — China's dominance looks rational at the global scale; from Fiji's doorstep it is devastating

> "You've been working toward this room since Lecture 1. The ambition/participation paradox from week one — you just lived it. The question now is what you do with that."

## Step 7 — Exit Ticket and Personal Commitment (5 min)

Go around the room. Each student states one thing — not a policy recommendation, not an analysis — a personal response to the question:

> "What question are you leaving this course with that you didn't have at the start?"

Do not editorialize. After the last student, close:

> "That's the right place to end. The question is more important than the answer."

---

\newpage

# Facilitator Reference — Quick Decisions

| Situation | Response |
|---|---|
| Condition is ambiguous | Ask conditioner to restate precisely before plenary ends |
| Cascade loop risk | Resolve in one pass; announce final positions after single audit |
| L&D invoked during plenary | Politely hold — L&D is for bilateral period only |
| US shock question during bilateral | "That is a matter for your bilateral discussions." Stay in role. |
| Student enters wrong number in tool | Correct immediately; re-enter; note it verbally |
| Temperature doesn't move visibly after Round 2 | That is the lesson — name it in debrief |
| Coalition breaks down before Round 2 | Do not intervene — bilateral realignment is realistic |
| Someone refuses to submit a proposal | Enter BAU for that country; note it in debrief as a real COP dynamic |

---

# Facilitator Reference — Key Numbers

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| BAU temperature (tool) | ~2.65°C |
| 2°C target | Requires ~0.65°C reduction from BAU |
| US Shock A temperature effect | ~−0.12°C |
| China max leverage (10%/yr from 2025) | ~0.50°C |
| Fiji + Marshall Islands combined leverage | ~0.0001°C |
| Rest-of-World BAU (Russia/Indonesia/Saudi/Canada) | ~7 Gt additional |
| IPCC TCRE | 0.45°C per 1000 GtCO2 (range 0.27–0.63°C) |
| Burke et al. damage function optimum | ~13°C |

---

# Facilitator Reference — Proposal Record Sheet

*Use this to track proposals as countries present. Transfer to Quick Entry modal for model entry.*

| Country | Start year | Rate (%/yr) | Fallback rate | Condition | Transfer (\$B/yr) | Condition met? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Japan | | | | | | |
| EU | | | | | | |
| Brazil | | | | | | |
| China | | | | | | |
| India | | | | | | |
| Nigeria | | | | | | |
| Norway | | | | | | |
| Fiji | | | | | | |
| Marshall Islands | | | | | | |
| Vietnam | | | | | | |
| Mexico | | | | | | |
| **Net transfer total** | | | | | | |

*Net transfer total must equal zero before model entry. If not, identify the discrepancy and ask the relevant countries to reconcile before entry.*
